data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: Pharma stocks, BTU, CALM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched higher on Wednesday, shrugging off the latest escalation in global trade tensions after new U.S. tariffs took effect at midnight, prompting retaliatory measures from China and the European Union..

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) +8% – Shares gained after the airline beat Q1 earnings estimates, although it pulled full-year guidance amid a challenging macroeconomic backdrop. CEO Ed Bastian cited stalling growth due to global trade uncertainty and said the company is taking steps to protect margins and cash flow, including flat capacity growth in H2 and tighter cost controls. For Q2, Delta expects profitability between $1.5B and $2.0B, with EPS of $1.70 to $2.30 (midpoint $2.00) versus $2.29 consensus. The company also plans to repay at least $3B in debt this year.
  • Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) +4% – Shares rose after Piper Sandler upgraded the stock to Overweight from Neutral, citing strong category growth and solid execution. Analyst Korinne Wolfmeyer noted the business is “fairly well-insulated” from macro pressures, with Fragrance and Body Care among the fastest-growing beauty segments. She highlighted rising penetration in mass channels and set a price target of $35, implying a 37% upside from the last close.
  • Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) +17% – Shares extended gains after a 9.2% rise in the regular session, following President Trump’s executive orders aimed at reviving the U.S. coal industry. Trump said he would invoke the Defense Production Act to support coal mining, invest in next-generation coal technologies, and direct the DOJ to challenge state and local regulations impacting coal jobs. He linked the move to bolstering electricity output to maintain U.S. leadership in the AI race.
  • PacBio (PACB) +15% - Shares soared following the announcement of its preliminary Q1 2025 results, which were in line with company expectations despite challenges related to NIH funding and broader economic conditions. Contributing factors to this positive market response included record levels of consumable revenue, growing orders for the Vega systems due to their capabilities and pricing, and strategic cost-reduction measures aimed at lowering operating expenses by up to $50M annually. The company’s focus on high-impact initiatives, such as the adoption of HiFi sequencing and improving gross margins, alongside restructuring efforts to boost sales efficiency, reflects a concerted effort to achieve their financial target of positive cash flow by the end of 2027.
  • Simply Good Foods (SMPL) +9.5% – Shares gained after the company posted strong Q2 results, surpassing estimates on both lines, with revenue rising 15.2% Y/Y. Adjusted EBITDA grew to $68M from $57.8M. The company reaffirmed its FY25 outlook, projecting net sales growth of 8.5% to 10.5% (vs. estimated +9.43% Y/Y) and adjusted EBITDA growth of 4% to 6%, noting that the 53rd week in FY24 will be a ~2pp headwind to both metrics, which is already factored into the guidance.

Biggest stock losers

  • Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) -7% – Shares declined after the egg producer missed consensus expectations for FQ3, despite reporting a sharp increase in net sales and a more than threefold jump in profit, driven by a significant egg shortage that boosted prices. The company also announced a ~$258M deal to acquire Echo Lake Foods, a Wisconsin-based supplier of ready-to-eat egg and breakfast products. The deal, expected to close by the end of FY25, marks Cal-Maine’s entry into the high-growth, stable foodservice segment of the egg industry.
  • Pfizer (PFE) -3% – Shares of major pharmaceutical companies declined after President Trump said the U.S. would “very shortly” announce a major tariff on pharmaceuticals. AbbVie (ABBV) and AstraZeneca dropped over 4%, and Eli Lilly (LLY) and Merck (MRK) slid about 3%.

data.title: Confounding bond selloff continues as US10Y briefly tops 4.50% for first time since Feb
data.description:
A bruising selloff in longer-term U.S. Treasury yields extended into a third session on Wednesday, as traders continued to dump bonds in a move that has confounded market participants for an asset class that is largely seen as a safe-haven. 

Fixed-income has traditionally been seen as providing a refuge from crashing equity markets and spiking recession fears. Investors would normally snap up bonds for safety, driving yields lower.

But that hasn't happened.

The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) on Wednesday briefly reached as high as 4.52%, marking its highest level since late February. It was last up 9 basis points to 4.39%.  

The turmoil in the bond markets has further slammed sentiment at a time when U.S. stocks are facing their biggest crisis since the COVID-19 market crash in 2020.

"Perhaps even more alarmingly, US Treasury markets are also experiencing an incredibly aggressive selloff as we go to press, adding to the evidence that they’re losing their traditional haven status," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said on Wednesday.

Apollo Asset Management on Tuesday said one of the reasons for the bond selloff appears to be a broader unwinding of the basis trade by hedge funds, who are offloading their Treasury positions.

Meanwhile, there is speculation that foreign holders of U.S. bonds - especially the benchmark US10Y - could be dumping them as a counter to tariffs.

"Fixed-income investors may be starting to worry that the Chinese and other foreigners might start selling their U.S. Treasuries," Yardeni Research said on Tuesday.    

At the start of this week, the US10Y was sitting at 3.90% and has since exploded higher by 55 basis points. Also, similar activity is being observed in the 20-year yield (US20Y) and the 30-year yield (US30Y). 

“The Bond Market has entered the trade war. Yields up sharply as trade war with China accelerates. Best guess is the basis trade is causing yields to surge,” Kathy Jones, chief fixed income strategist at Charles Schwab said.  

Highlighted below is a quick snapshot on where Treasury yields sit across the curve:

  • U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) is at 3.78%.
  • U.S. 5 Year Treasury yield (US5Y) is at 4.00%.
  • U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) is at 4.39%. 
  • U.S. 20 Year Treasury yield (US20Y) is at 4.89%.
  • U.S. 30 Year Treasury yield (US30Y) is at 4.82%. 

Treasury and Fixed Income ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: Home Depot and Lowe's continue to slide amid Trump's tariff policies and spike in borrowing costs
data.description:
Home Depot (NYSE:HD) and Lowe's (NYSE:LOW) swung lower on Wednesday amid concerns about consumer discretionary spending and with investors eyeing longer-term Treasury yields that continue to track higher. While the two companies are better insulated from tariff costs than many retailers, the economic backdrop created by the Trump Administration's policies is working against them.

Notably, Treasury yields moved higher this week for maturities stretching from one year to thirty years, which impacts mortgage rates and loan rates for consumers looking at home improvement projects.

The jump in Treasury yields this week will quickly push up borrowing costs across the financial system, according to economists. Higher rates also increase the risk of a recession in the U.S., which would dampen investor enthusiasm for home improvement stocks. While the Federal Reserve can take swift action on short-term interest rates, the central bank has less impact on the longer end of the yield curve.

Shares of Home Depot (NYSE:HD) fell 2.2% in early trading on Wednesday and are down 14% over the last six weeks. Lowe's Companies (NYSE:LOW) swung 1.8% lower at the open and is down 16% over the last six weeks. Both home improvement stocks have a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of Hold.

data.title: Bath & Body Works remains teen's favorite fragrance brand -- Piper Sandler
data.description:
Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) continued to hold the top spot as the favorite fragrance for teen females, beating out luxury rivals like Dior, Chanel, and Marc Jacobs, and is now becoming one of the top shopping destinations within this highly-coveted demographic.

With a healthy and growing market in two of the fastest growing categories in beauty --fragrance and body care -- a vertically-integrated structure, and accessible price-points, Piper Sandler upgraded Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) to Overweight from Neutral, driving shares more than 3% higher at Wednesday’s open, putting an end a four-day losing streak.

According to Piper Sandler’s semi-annual teen survey, spending on beauty reached a new high with fragrance again taking the top spot. Spending among female teens returned to double-digit growth with the core beauty wallet reaching the highest level yet of $374.

“Fragrance continues to be a stand-out, with annual spend up 22% year-over-year for females and up 44% yoy for males,” Piper’s Korinne Wolfmeyer found.

“[These] results bode fairly well for [BBWI], with fragrance spend strong and teens continuing to rank the brand highly, as well as management’s efforts to expand their beauty presence clearly resonating. At the same time, we see a fairly insulated model from the current macro debates,” Wolfmeyer adds.

Besides being a favorite fragrance among teens, Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) is also gaining popularity as a favorite shopping destination. After not even cracking the top 10 in over 6 years, BBWI shot up to the third favorite spot, behind only Sephora and Ulta (ULTA). This underscores effective efforts by BBWI’s new leadership to focus on expanding the beauty assortment and making the storefronts engaging for beauty consumers.   

With less than 10% of its merchandise sourced from China—and the majority produced in the U.S.—BBWI faces minimal exposure to tariff risk, according to Piper Sandler’s Wolfmeyer. Management has already factored in a limited impact from initial tariffs (excluding China’s newly announced 84% retaliatory tariffs on Wednesday), and Piper’s forecasts also reflect a conservative stance. Nevertheless, the firm sees sufficient upside to raise its revenue and EPS estimates for FY25 and FY26 by ~0.4% to 0.5%.

data.title: A US-built iPhone could cost 90% more, BofA says
data.description:
Following yesterday's comments from the White House that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) could build an iPhone in the U.S., Bank of America said an endeavor is “technically possible,” but it would come at an enormous cost.

Shares rose 3% in early trading on Wednesday, erasing premarket losses.

“In our opinion, iPhone cost can increase 25% purely on higher labor cost in the U.S.,” analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote in a note to clients. “On top of that, if Apple had to pay reciprocal tariffs to import sub-assemblies into the U.S., we see the total cost of an iPhone increasing 90%+.”

Mohan, who has a Buy rating and $250 price target on Apple, took his estimates based on the iPhone 16 Pro Max.

Assuming Apple could find the labor in the U.S. to assemble iPhones, Mohan said that a “significant” portion of the sub-assemblies would still need to be manufactured elsewhere, assembled in China and then imported to the U.S. “This is because, while it may be possible to move final assembly to the U.S., moving the entire iPhone supply chain would be a much bigger undertaking and would likely take many years, if even possible,” Mohan explained.

Despite efforts to diversify its supply chain geographically, Apple still relies on China for a significant portion of its manufacturing needs, specifically iPhones. Investment firm Needham recently estimated that Apple's fiscal 2025 earnings could be negatively impacted by 28% or more in a trade war between China and the U.S. unless it was given an exemption.

Aside from China, Apple and its partners manufacture iPhones, AirPods, Apple Watches, Macs and iPads in several countries, including India, Vietnam and Brazil. All of these countries have also been hit with tariffs by President Trump.

Apple releases its second quarter fiscal 2025 financial results on May 1. A consensus estimate expects Apple to report earnings per share of $1.61 on revenue of $94.04B.

data.title: Federal Reserve has time and space for next moves, SF Fed's Daly says
data.description:
Hard economic data is still solid and "monetary policy is in a good place, allowing us the time and space to be deliberate with our next moves," San Francisco Fed President Mary C. Daly said in a social media post.

"Businesses, households, and policymakers face elevated uncertainty," Daly wrote in a post on LinkedIn. "My approach is to look constantly but tread slowly."

Many businesses in the San Francisco Fed district say they're adopting a similar attitude. At a CEO roundtable in Utah that she attended on Tuesday, Daly said she saw "resiliency, planning, and agility."

"Now isn't the time to jump to any conclusions on where the economy is heading," she said. "That is a recipe for making a mistake."

On Tuesday, she said rate policy is "modestly restrictive" and that tariffs could reaccelerate inflation.

data.title: ECB's Holzmann sees no need for rate cuts until tariff-induced uncertainty fades - report
data.description:
European Central Bank Governing Council member Robert Holzmann reportedly said Wednesday that there is no need for further interest-rate cuts should tariff-driven uncertainty continue to cloud the economic outlook.

With U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive and unpredictable global trade policies, it is now virtually impossible to predict whether inflation in the eurozone will head toward the ECB's 2% target, Holzmann said in an interview in Vienna, Austria, Bloomberg reported.

The Governing Council is set to meet next week to decide what to do with borrowing costs, though many policymakers likely won't jibe with Holzmann's hawkish tone. The ECB reduced its key rate for a sixth time last week, though Holzmann stood alone in opposing the cut. Minutes from that meeting revealed officials were considering either another cut or a hold in April.

But that was before Trump's tariffs kicked in, a move that's driving an escalating trade war and roiling financial markets. 

Even so, “we should move extremely carefully and certainly shouldn’t accelerate if the outlook is this uncertain.” Holzmann said., noting that the ECB's rate is already low after a flurry of cuts.

At the end of March, still prior to Trump's April 2 Liberation Day announcement, Bloomberg reported that multiple ECB officials were contemplating a rate-pause this month.

data.title: Gevo, Future Energy Global sign SAF carbon credit offtake agreement
data.description:
Gevo (NASDAQ:GEVO) +2.9% in early trading Wednesday after saying it signed a multi-year offtake agreement with Future Energy Global for carbon abatement attributes from sustainable aviation fuel, in which FEG will acquire Scope 1 and Scope 3 emissions credits from 10M gal/year of fuel to be produced at Gevo's planned alcohol-to-jet SAF facility.

Gevo (NASDAQ:GEVO) said it expects the agreement will support financing for the construction of its ATJ-60 facility, which has secured a $1.63B conditional loan guarantee from the U.S. Department of Energy.

The Lake Preston, South Dakota, facility is designed to produce 60M gal/year of SAF at costs comparable to conventional jet fuel but with lower carbon emissions.

Gevo (GEVO) "has always planned to leverage SAF market economics to scale our business, and a Book and Claim market that enables the trading of SAF environmental attributes can accelerate SAF production even faster," CEO Patrick Gruber said.

The 'Book and Claim' approach separates the Scope 1 and Scope 3 attributes from physical fuel, reducing transportation costs and enabling a global SAF market; the aviation industry has targeted net-zero CO2 emissions by 2050, and SAF is expected to contribute roughly two-thirds of the necessary emissions reduction.

data.title: Monster Beverage says Spruce Point short report 'false' and 'misleading'
data.description:
Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) said a short report from Spruce Point contains "false and misleading" characterizations of the company.

"The document released by a self-interested activist short seller is filled with and based on inaccuracies and aspersions that appear to be designed to distort the Company’s record and share price for its own gain," Monster Co-CEOs Rodney Sacks and Hilton Schlosberg said in a statement on Wednesday. 

The Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) response comes after the Spruce Point short report was issued on Tuesday. Shares of Monster rose 2% on Wednesday after falling 3.7% on Tuesday.

Monster (MNST) added that the short report doesn't have any credibility and the company doesn't plan to have any further comment.

data.title: SNDL to buy 32 stores from 1CM
data.description:
• SNDL (NASDAQ:SNDL) and 1CM (CSE: EPIC) signed a deal for SNDL to buy 32 cannabis retail stores operating under the Cost Cannabis and T Cannabis banners in Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan.
  • SNDL said it will acquire the 1CM Stores for a cash value of C$32.2M.
  • This acquisition is expected to bring SNDL's total owned and franchised cannabis retail store count to 219.
  • Deal is expected to close by the end of the third quarter of 2025, the company said.
